source_metadata.title_raw: Hilda Cantu Montoy - I-19-2019 - November 15, 2019 - Fresno

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 15, 2019

Hilda Cantu Montoy
City Attorney
City of Madera
2440 Tulare St, Suite 410
Fresno, CA 93721

Re:     Your Request for Informal Assistance
         Our File No. 1-19-209

Dear Ms. Montoy;

         This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

         Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

         Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.² Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

         Under the Act, may a Madera City Councilmember take part in governmental decisions
involving Project Labor Agreements with unions, given that he is a salaried employee of a non-
profit organization that advocates on behalf of unions?

CONCLUSION

         In general, the Councilmember will likely be disqualified from taking part in decisions
involving Project Labor Agreements, given the nexus between such decisions and the work he is
paid to do as a salaried employee of an organization that advocates on behalf of unions.

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-19-209
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The Madera City Council is contemplating looking into Project Labor Agreements
("PLAs"). A PLA is a pre-hire collective bargaining agreement with one or more labor
organizations that establishes terms and conditions of employment for a specific construction
project or projects. PLAs are also known as Project Stabilization Agreements.

Examples of the City Council's potential consideration of PLAs in the near future could
involve:

1. A workshop on the subject of Project Labor Agreements;
2. A meeting to determine parameters for PLAs such as adopting a resolution or ordinance for
processing PLAs; and
3. A meeting to consider approval of a PLA with a labor union(s).

Councilmember Santos Garcia began serving on the Madera City Council in December
2018. He has been employed by the Fresno-Madera-Tulare-Kings Central Labor Council ("CLC")
since February 2016 as Labor Organizer/Community Organizer. The CLC is a 501(c)(5) nonprofit
organization and has three (3) employees: the Executive Director, Accounting Manager, and
Labor/Organizer/Community Organizer. The CLC is an umbrella organization for 55 unions. The
CLC is an information hub and advocacy organization that works on behalf of its affiliated unions.
The organization's general purpose is to maintain a central body for shared information and
communication amongst affiliated unions. Affiliated unions pay membership dues to CLC, but
those dues are not earmarked for any purpose. There are no other fees that CLC derives from the
unions or their members. CLC would normally advocate for PLAs in all of its jurisdictions.

Councilmember Garcia is not an officer or director of the CLC. His job duties involve
outreach on behalf of the Executive Director to CLC's affiliated unions and "community allies."
The outreach includes phone calls, emails, and in-person appearances to organize affiliated unions
and community allies around shared goals and events. Essentially, he maintains relationships on
behalf of the Executive Director with a variety of organizations that CLC works with on union
issues. He is a salaried employee with no bonuses or performance-based incentives. He maintains
the same salary regardless of union membership within CLC or any fluctuating affiliations. If a
PLA is entered into by the City, it will be with a union or unions who may or may not be members
of the CLC; the PLA would not be with the CLC and would not be with Councilmember Garcia.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100 of the Act, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." Section 87103 specifies that "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family," or on certain economic interests, including "[a]ny source
of income . . . aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to,

File No. I-19-209
Page No. 3

[or] received by, the official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made."
(Section 87103.)

Without a specific PLA-related decision to analyze, it cannot be definitively stated whether
Councilmember Garcia is disqualified under the Act. Such a determination is made on a decision-
by-decision basis on the facts available for each specific decision. However, under
Regulation 18702.3's "nexus test," any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a source of
income to a public official or the official's spouse is material if the decision will achieve, defeat,
aid, or hinder a purpose or goal of the source and the official or the official's spouse receives or is
promised the income for achieving the purpose or goal. (Regulation 18702.3(h).) The rationale for
the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose that may be
advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the private employer is
benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Garza Advice Letter,
No. A-17-207; Tran Advice Letter, No. A-16-024; Maltbie Advice Letter, No. A-15-243.)

Councilmember Garcia is one of three members of the CLC. The CLC is an information hub
and advocacy organization that works on behalf of its affiliated unions. The CLC ordinarily
advocates for PLAs in all the organization's jurisdictions. Although Councilmember Garcia does
not receive a "performance bonus" for his work for CLC, the very nature of the CLC and
Councilmember Garcia's work for the organization appears to be closely tied to advancing the
interests of affiliated unions and achieving beneficial working conditions for them. Accordingly, it
is likely that in many cases, Councilmember Garcia will be disqualified from taking part in PLA-
related decisions under the Act's nexus test.

It is worth noting, however, that in some cases Councilmember Garcia may be permitted,
under the Act, to take part in PLA-related decisions if properly segmented. Under Regulation
18706, an agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial interest, to allow
participation by the official, provided all of the following conditions apply:

(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into separate
decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official has a
disqualifying financial interest;

(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the other
decisions;

(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and a final
decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's participation in any
way; and

(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, the
disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or otherwise
financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified.

(Regulation 18706(a).)

Given the lack of a specific contract to analyze, we cannot analyze and determine whether a
PLA, or Councilmember Garcia's taking part in a PLA-related decision, would be prohibited under

File No. 1-19-209
Page No. 4

Section 1090.³ If the City Council considers a specific PLA or similar contract in the future, it may seek formal advice by following the procedure outlined at http://www.fppc.ca.gov/advice/formal-advice.html.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:sal

³ We note, however, that Section 1091 provides, "[a]n officer shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract entered into by a body or board of which the officer is a member within the meaning of this article if the officer has only a remote interest in the contract and if the fact of that interest is disclosed to the body or board of which the officer is a member and noted in its official records, and thereafter the body or board authorizes, approves, or ratifies the contract in good faith by a vote of its membership sufficient for the purpose without counting the vote or votes of the officer or member with the remote interest." (Section 1091(a).) As used in Section 1091, a "remote interest" includes that of an officer or employee of a nonprofit entity exempt from taxation pursuant to Section 501(c)(5). (Section 1091(b)(1).)

profit organization that advocates on behalf of unions?
